subject: ap-comparative-government
theme: The structure of political institutions — how executives, legislatures, and judiciaries are designed and how they interact — determines the real distribution of power within a political system, which often differs sharply from the formal constitutional description.

core_concepts.title: Parliamentary vs. Presidential vs. Semi-Presidential
core_concepts.detail: Three executive system types appear in the course. Parliamentary systems (UK) fuse executive and legislative authority: the prime minister is drawn from the legislature and must maintain its confidence; the legislature can remove the executive through a vote of no confidence. Presidential systems (Mexico and Nigeria) separate the executive and legislature, each with fixed terms; the executive cannot be removed by an ordinary legislative vote — only impeachment. Semi-presidential systems (Russia) have both a popularly elected president and a prime minister accountable to the legislature; the president nominates the PM and holds far more real power. A common MCQ trap: Russia is NOT parliamentary even though it has a prime minister. The UK prime minister is accountable to Parliament; Russia's president is elected independently and can dismiss the PM.

core_concepts.title: Executive Structure Country by Country
core_concepts.detail: Each country's executive has distinctive features. UK: constitutional monarchy — the monarch is ceremonial head of state; the PM (head of government, de facto commander in chief) must maintain Commons majority; PM can call elections and sets foreign policy. Russia: dual executive — the president is head of state and commander in chief, the PM is head of government, but the president dominates. China: the president combines three roles — state president, CPC General Secretary, and Central Military Commission chair — leadership changes happen inside the party, not through public elections. Iran: dual authority — the Supreme Leader outranks all institutions (commands military, appoints judiciary, controls vetting bodies); the elected president manages civil service and foreign policy but is subordinate. Mexico: single-term (one six-year term) elected president; serves as both head of state and head of government; no reelection. Nigeria: presidential system mirroring Mexico's structure; president is head of state, head of government, and commander in chief.

core_concepts.title: Unicameral vs. Bicameral Legislatures
core_concepts.detail: China and Iran have unicameral legislatures. China's National People's Congress (NPC) is constitutionally the most powerful institution but in practice rubber-stamps decisions already made by the Politburo Standing Committee; the NPC meets once a year and almost never rejects legislation. Iran's Majles is directly elected but operates under oversight of the Guardian Council, which can veto legislation for incompatibility with Islamic law. Mexico, Nigeria, Russia, and the UK are all bicameral. Russia's Federation Council (upper house) is appointed, not elected; it approves budgets, treaties, and judicial nominees. The UK's House of Lords is appointed (by the monarch on the PM's recommendation); it can delay but not permanently block Commons legislation. Nigeria's Senate holds impeachment powers requiring a two-thirds majority. A common error: students claim Russia's and UK's upper houses are equivalent — they serve different functions and reflect different power structures.

core_concepts.title: Real vs. Formal Power: China and Iran
core_concepts.detail: The most heavily tested institutional contrast in Unit 2 is the gap between formal constitutional authority and real governing power. In China, the NPC is constitutionally the supreme institution, but actual power rests with the Politburo Standing Committee — a small inner circle of 7 senior CPC leaders who make all major decisions. The NPC's legitimizing function (lending public approval to pre-made decisions) is what matters for the exam. The Standing Committee of the NPC handles legislative duties when the full NPC is not in session. In Iran, the elected president appears powerful because citizens vote for them, but the Supreme Leader commands the military, appoints the head of the judiciary, controls the Guardian Council and Expediency Council, and sets overall state policy. The Guardian Council (12 members: 6 Islamic jurists appointed by Supreme Leader + 6 civil lawyers approved by Majles) vets all legislation for Islamic compliance and screens all candidates for elected office.

core_concepts.title: Judicial Systems and Independence
core_concepts.detail: Judicial independence — courts' ability to rule against the executive and legislature without consequences — varies sharply across the six countries. UK: rule of law; Supreme Court serves as final court of appeals, protects human rights, and adjudicates devolution disputes; common law tradition. China: rule by law — courts serve the CPC, which controls most judicial appointments; no real constraint on state power. Iran: the judiciary must apply Islamic Sharia law; the head of judiciary is appointed by the Supreme Leader; judges must be trained in Islamic jurisprudence. Mexico: in transition toward independence; Supreme Court has formal judicial review power; justices nominated by president, confirmed by Senate for 15-year terms. Nigeria: judicial review exists formally; Sharia courts operate in northern states under federalism; ongoing efforts to reduce corruption. Russia: courts formally have judicial review but in practice are used by the government to target opposition rather than constrain it; judges nominated by president, confirmed by Federation Council.

core_concepts.title: Executive Removal and Term Limits
core_concepts.detail: How executives are removed is a key comparative question. UK: vote of no confidence by the House of Commons — the PM and cabinet must resign if they lose majority support; the PM can also be replaced if their party removes them as leader. Russia: the State Duma can pass a no-confidence vote in the prime minister (the president can also dismiss the PM unilaterally); presidential impeachment requires State Duma + Federation Council + Supreme Court confirmation. Mexico: presidential impeachment proceedings exist but are extremely rare; the single term limit makes removal less politically salient. Nigeria: the National Assembly (both chambers, two-thirds majority) can impeach the president for gross misconduct — this is one of Nigeria's most distinctive institutional features. China: the NPC formally can remove the president, but leadership transitions occur through internal CPC mechanisms. Iran: the Assembly of Experts can remove the Supreme Leader (this almost never happens); the Majles can impeach the elected president by simple majority. Term limits advantages: check executive power, prevent dictators, force focus on governing. Disadvantages: create lame-duck periods, interrupt policy continuity, waste experienced leadership.

exam_tip: The three highest-yield Unit 2 questions on the AP exam test: (1) China's NPC vs. Politburo Standing Committee — the NPC is constitutionally supreme but the Politburo Standing Committee holds real power; (2) Iran's Supreme Leader vs. elected president — the Supreme Leader outranks the elected president in every domain that matters (military, judiciary, vetting bodies); (3) vote of no confidence (parliamentary/UK) vs. impeachment (presidential/Nigeria) — these are not interchangeable removal mechanisms and the exam will test whether you know which country uses which.
